content.text: OkHttp includes SSE support in its core artifact — no separate dependency is needed. Add the OkHttp BOM to ensure consistent versions across the OkHttp family:
Define Kotlin data classes that mirror Claude's streaming event shapes. You only need to decode the fields you use — Gson ignores unknown fields by default.
provides four callbacks. Emit events to a
can be collected by the ViewModel. The listener callbacks are invoked on OkHttp's internal dispatcher thread, not the main thread.
Wrap the OkHttp setup in a client class that returns a
. The caller never touches OkHttp directly — it only collects the typed Flow.

package — no separate SSE artifact is needed for OkHttp 4.x.
An alternative pattern avoids the separate listener class by using
, which converts callback-based APIs into cold Flows. The Flow automatically closes the EventSource when the collector cancels.
and accumulates token text into a
that Compose observes. The ViewModel also manages cancellation — when the user navigates away, the Flow collection is cancelled, which triggers
and closes the underlying OkHttp EventSource.
The examples above show the Claude API key in the client — this is only appropriate for backend services. In an Android app,
never embed the API key in the APK
. Route requests through your own backend that holds the key and authenticates the Android user with a session token:
Adding Interceptors for Logging and Retry
OkHttp interceptors run on every request, including SSE connections. Use
in debug builds to inspect the SSE byte stream. For production, add a retry interceptor for transient network errors:

import okhttp3.logging.HttpLoggingInterceptor

val loggingInterceptor = HttpLoggingInterceptor().apply {
    level = if (BuildConfig.DEBUG)
        HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.BODY
    else
        HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.NONE
}

// Simple retry interceptor for 503 / network errors (NOT idempotency issues)
val retryInterceptor = Interceptor { chain ->
    var attempt = 0
    var response = chain.proceed(chain.request())
    while (!response.isSuccessful && response.code == 503 && attempt < 2) {
        response.close()
        attempt++
        Thread.sleep(500L * attempt)
        response = chain.proceed(chain.request())
    }
    response
}

val httpClient = OkHttpClient.Builder()
    .readTimeout(120, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
    .connectTimeout(15, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
    .addInterceptor(loggingInterceptor)
    .addInterceptor(retryInterceptor)
    .build()
will print the entire SSE response body as it streams — useful for debugging event shapes but very verbose. Reduce to
if you only need to verify connection setup.
Default read timeout cancels the stream:
OkHttp's default read timeout is 10 seconds. Claude responses can stream for much longer. Always set
Listener callbacks on the wrong thread:
is called on OkHttp's internal thread pool. Never update UI directly from the callback. Always emit to a Flow or coroutine channel and collect on Main.
Not cancelling the EventSource on navigation:
reference outside the Flow and the user navigates away, the HTTP connection stays open. The
pattern handles this automatically when the ViewModel scope cancels.
When Claude returns a 4xx (e.g., 401 for bad API key, 429 for rate limit), OkHttp calls
and a null throwable. Always check both parameters to reconstruct a useful error message.
is correct for the raw event stream (each token is a discrete event).
is correct for the accumulated text that the UI displays. Convert by accumulating in the ViewModel as shown above.
